title: Is Taking Core STEM Classes Online a Recipe for Getting Cooked in University?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are choosing your senior year courses. You need calculus to get into the engineering program you are targeting. Your high school offers it, but the teacher has a reputation for being brutal. An online version is available. It is self-paced. The reviews say it is easier. Your friends are taking it and getting A's. The temptation to take the easier path is strong. You will still have calculus on your transcript. The college will not know the difference. Will they? The answer is that they might not know from your transcript, but you will know when you arrive on campus and your university calculus course assumes you learned things the online course did not teach. Here is how to think about the trade-off between online and in-person STEM courses in high school.

What Online Courses Often Miss

Online STEM courses vary enormously in quality. Some are excellent. They are taught by experienced instructors, require rigorous problem sets, and prepare students well for university work. Others are essentially credit recovery. They cover the minimum content required to award a grade, assess understanding superficially, and leave students with a false sense of mastery. The problem is that the two types look the same on a high school transcript.

The differences become visible on a university campus. Students who took rigorous in-person STEM courses have usually developed several skills beyond the content itself. They have learned to work through long, multi-step problems under time pressure. They have learned to show their work clearly enough that someone else can follow their reasoning. They have learned to ask questions when they are confused and to persist through problems that take more than a few minutes to solve. These are not separate from learning calculus. They are part of learning calculus. An online course that emphasizes multiple-choice assessments and allows unlimited retakes may not develop these skills at all.

University STEM courses assume you have these skills. The professor will not reteach how to structure a solution. They will not slow down to make sure you can manage your time on a fifty-minute exam. They expect that your prerequisite courses prepared you not just in content but in academic maturity. If your online course did not build that maturity, you will spend your first semester catching up on skills your classmates already have.

When Online STEM Courses Make Sense

Online courses are not inherently inferior. In some situations, they are the best available option. If your high school does not offer a course you need, an online course may be your only path to taking it. If the in-person teacher is genuinely inadequate, not just difficult, an online course with a strong instructor may provide better preparation. If you have demonstrated self-discipline and can learn independently, the flexibility of an online format may suit you.

The key is to evaluate the specific online course, not the category. Ask these questions before you enroll. Who teaches the course, and what are their qualifications? How are students assessed? Are there timed exams that require you to demonstrate understanding without notes? Are there free-response problems that require you to show your work? How much interaction do students have with the instructor? A course with rigorous assessments, an experienced instructor, and opportunities for feedback is a different experience from a course that is essentially a series of videos followed by multiple-choice quizzes.

If you take an online STEM course, supplement it aggressively. Work through extra problems beyond what is assigned. Find a tutor or a study group. Take practice exams under timed conditions. Assume that the course is teaching you the minimum and that you need to build the rest yourself. The students who succeed after taking online prerequisites are the ones who treated the online course as a starting point, not a complete education.

What Universities Actually Think

Admissions officers are aware that online courses vary in quality. A transcript that shows a pattern of taking the hardest available courses in person and supplementing with online courses for subjects not offered at your school is impressive. A transcript that shows you took the easy online version of every course your school offered in person raises questions. It suggests you avoided challenge rather than seeking it.

Some universities ask on their applications whether you took any courses online or through credit recovery. Be honest. The dishonesty of hiding it is worse than the slight negative impression of having taken it. If you are asked to explain, focus on what you learned and how you supplemented the course, not on why you avoided the harder version.

The Bottom Line

Online STEM courses can be a legitimate path, but they require caution. Many do not build the problem-solving stamina, exam skills, and academic maturity that university courses assume. Before you enroll, evaluate the specific course's rigor, assessments, and instructor interaction. If you take the online option, supplement with extra practice and independent study. A strong grade in a rigorous in-person course is better preparation than a perfect grade in an easy online course. The goal is not just to get the credit. The goal is to be ready for what comes next.
